items.myth: To multiply two matrices, they must have exactly the same shape.
items.reality: Matrix multiplication only requires that the number of columns of the left matrix equals the number of rows of the right matrix; the resulting matrix keeps the outer dimensions, which are often different from each input.
items.example: A 2 x 3 matrix can be multiplied by a 3 x 5 matrix to produce a 2 x 5 matrix, even though none of the shapes are identical.

items.myth: Matrix multiplication always commutes, so AB is the same as BA whenever both products are defined.
items.reality: Matrix multiplication is generally not commutative; AB and BA usually represent different linear transformations and can have different shapes or values.
items.example: In 2D geometry, rotating and then scaling along the x-axis gives a different result from scaling first and then rotating; the corresponding matrices R and S satisfy RS != SR.

items.myth: Each entry of AB depends only on the entries Ai,j and Bi,j at the same position.
items.reality: Each entry Ci,j in the product depends on an entire row of A and an entire column of B via a dot product, so changing a single element in A or B can affect a full row or column of C.
items.example: For C = AB with A 2 x 2 and B 2 x 2, C0,0 = A0,0 * B0,0 + A0,1 * B1,0, which involves both entries in row 0 of A and both entries in column 0 of B.
